content.assessment: A structural grade-split model would require reclassifying school units and their legal status, since maintainer authority is currently centralized in district centers with directors appointed by the Minister rather than elected locally (F-live-legal_and_governance-02). No finding directly addresses the legal mechanics of splitting a single school's grade structure across sites, but the same 2016 restructuring architecture (59 district centers subordinate to a central agency) would presumably govern which entity manages the retained lower-grade sites and the consolidated upper-grade site, raising questions of accountability fragmentation. The lack of a dedicated legal closure procedure for demographic decline (F-live-legal_and_governance-04) is relevant here too, since decisions to convert (rather than close) a village school would likely proceed through the same undocumented administrative discretion, absent new statutory grounding for a
opportunities: Could be incorporated into the 2026 restructuring's stated goal of restoring some school-director and municipal input, if grade-split governance is explicitly designed into that reform (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
strengths: Retaining a village-based lower-grade site partially preserves local accountability structures that would otherwise be lost entirely under a full closure
threats: Absent codified protections, a future administration could reverse the grade-split unilaterally through the same discretionary administrative mechanism used for current closures, with no statutory barrier (F-live-legal_and_governance-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
weaknesses: No existing statutory closure/conversion procedure exists to legally distinguish a grade-split conversion from a full 'temporary closure' decision, leaving conversions similarly vulnerable to ad hoc administrative discretion (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)

Centralized director appointment and advisory-only district councils mean local communities would have no formal legal authority over how a grade-split is designed or later reversed (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
